stem: Evaluate \( \displaystyle \int_{0}^{[[c]]} \frac{x^{[[n]]}}{x^{[[n]]} + ([[c]] - x)^{[[n]]}}\, dx \). (Hint: apply the reflection substitution \( x \mapsto [[c]] - x \).)
solution: Let \( I \) be the integral. The reflection (king's) property \( \int_{0}^{[[c]]} g(x)\,dx = \int_{0}^{[[c]]} g([[c]] - x)\,dx \) replaces the integrand by \( \frac{([[c]] - x)^{[[n]]}}{([[c]] - x)^{[[n]]} + x^{[[n]]}} \). Adding the two forms, the integrands sum to 1, so \( 2I = \int_{0}^{[[c]]} 1\, dx = [[c]] \) and \( I = \frac{[[c]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( 2I \), the total interval length, not \( I = [[c]]/2 \) after dividing by 2.
This wrongly assumes the exponent \( [[n]] \) scales the answer; king's property makes the result independent of \( [[n]] \).
This halves the answer a second time.
This doubles the interval length instead of halving it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \displaystyle \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \frac{([[a]])\sin^{[[n]]}(x)}{\sin^{[[n]]}(x) + \cos^{[[n]]}(x)}\, dx \). (Hint: apply the reflection substitution \( x \mapsto \pi/2 - x \), which swaps \( \sin \) and \( \cos \).)
solution: Let \( I \) be the integral without the factor \( [[a]] \). Reflecting \( x \mapsto \pi/2 - x \) swaps \( \sin \) and \( \cos \), turning the integrand into \( \frac{\cos^{[[n]]}(x)}{\cos^{[[n]]}(x)+\sin^{[[n]]}(x)} \). Adding the two forms, the integrands sum to 1, so \( 2I=\int_0^{\pi/2}1\,dx=\pi/2 \) and \( I=\pi/4 \), independent of \( [[n]] \). Scaled by \( [[a]] \), the answer is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to halve \( \pi/2 \) down to \( \pi/4 \) before scaling by \( [[a]] \).
This halves the correct value \( \pi/4 \) a second time.
This wrongly assumes the exponent \( [[n]] \) scales the answer; king's property makes the result independent of \( [[n]] \).
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Evaluate \( \displaystyle \int_{0}^{\pi} ([[a]])\,x\sin(x)\, dx \). (Hint: apply the reflection substitution \( x \mapsto \pi - x \) to the integral \( \int_0^\pi x\sin(x)\,dx \), using that \( \sin(\pi - x) = \sin(x) \).)
solution: Let \( J = \int_0^\pi x\sin(x)\,dx \). Reflecting \( x \mapsto \pi - x \) gives \( J = \int_0^\pi (\pi - x)\sin(x)\,dx = \pi\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\,dx - J \), so \( 2J = \pi \cdot 2 \) and \( J = \pi \). Scaled by \( [[a]] \), the integral equals \( [[a]]\pi = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct value.
This forgets that \( \int_0^\pi \sin(x)\,dx = 2 \), using \( \pi \) in its place before halving.
This doubles the correct value.
This uses a quarter of \( \pi \) instead of the correct \( \pi \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx = [[K]] \). Using the reflection property \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\,dx = \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f([[a]]+[[b]]-x)\,dx \), evaluate \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f([[a]]+[[b]]-x)\, dx \).
solution: The reflection (king's) property states that substituting \( x \mapsto [[a]]+[[b]]-x \) leaves a definite integral over \( [[[a]],[[b]]] \) unchanged. So \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f([[a]]+[[b]]-x)\, dx = \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} f(x)\, dx = [[K]] \), with no computation needed beyond recognizing the invariance.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the reflection substitution flips the sign of the integral.
This wrongly assumes the reflection substitution doubles the integral.
This wrongly assumes the reflection substitution halves the integral.
This wrongly assumes reflecting the integrand about the interval's midpoint sends the integral to 0.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This wrongly adds the interval width to the invariant value.
